Q1: Being 29 and juggling multiple responsibilities, I had to be pretty strategic about my study routine. I typically blocked out about 30 hours a week, usually in longer sessions when I could really dive deep into hands-on practice. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so I'd often set up practical exercises where I could apply concepts physically or through simulations. My routine wasn't always perfect, but I tried to protect my study blocks even when life got chaotic.
Q2: Honestly, my pattern varied quite a bit depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I'd try to maintain some consistency, but with 16 online courses to manage, deadlines would sometimes stack up and force me into intensive study bursts. Some weeks I'd be ahead of schedule, other weeks I'd be scrambling. The anxiety of falling behind actually pushed me to be more disciplined, even when I didn't feel like it.
Q3: Usually it was either to check on upcoming deadlines or to grab materials I needed for my hands-on study sessions. I logged in pretty frequently—probably about 21 times during the course—because I needed to stay on top of announcements and any changes. Sometimes my peer study group would mention something was posted, and I'd jump on to check it out. The fear of missing something important kept me coming back.
Q4: Yes, eventually logging in became almost automatic, like checking my phone in the morning. It happened gradually as I realized the LMS was where all the critical information lived. After connecting with some peers through the platform, I had more reason to check in regularly for discussions and group coordination. It became part of my daily rhythm, even if I wasn't always completing tasks while there.
Q5: The video content was incredibly valuable to me—I watched over 100 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace. I also found peer interactions really helpful; connecting with 37 different classmates gave me different perspectives and study support. Discussion posts, even though I only made a couple myself, helped me see how others were approaching problems. These resources felt practical and applicable.
Q6: I'll admit my assignment completion rate was pretty low—around 13%—which doesn't look great on paper. I often delayed written assignments because I was prioritizing understanding the material through practice first. Some reading materials I'd skim or skip if I felt I could learn the concept better through doing. It wasn't that they weren't valuable; I just had to triage based on my learning style and time constraints.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to real-world application or exam preparation. If I could physically engage with the material or practice a skill, I was all in. I also valued activities that helped me connect with peers because that support network was crucial for managing my anxiety. Anything that felt like busywork without clear purpose got deprioritized.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With required tasks, there was external pressure that helped override my tendency to procrastinate. Optional ones required more internal motivation, which was harder to summon when I was already stressed. But sometimes optional peer discussions were more valuable than required readings, so I learned to be selective rather than dismissive.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download materials and work with them on my commute or during breaks at work. I kept physical notes and created my own practice exercises that I could work through without needing internet access. I also used textbooks and external resources to supplement what was provided—the LMS was just the starting point for me.
Q10: There were many times I'd be deeply engaged in learning but the LMS would show nothing. I'd take one concept from a video and spend hours practicing it with physical materials or real-world applications. Study sessions with my peer support group often happened through other platforms or in person. My best learning moments were often when I was away from the screen, working things out with my hands.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then work with that material for days without logging back in. My high watch time reflects initial engagement, but the real learning continued offline. I'd revisit my notes repeatedly, test myself, and practice applications. The LMS capture was just the tip of the iceberg.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became really difficult, usually when my anxiety spiked or when multiple deadlines collided. My attendance dropped to about 62% partly because of these overwhelming moments. Life responsibilities as a 29-year-old sometimes demanded attention I couldn't postpone. During those times, I relied heavily on my resilience and peer support to push through rather than give up entirely.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just couldn't engage deeply. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between thorough engagement and just staying afloat. Confusion about certain topics would make me avoid those sections temporarily, even though I knew I'd have to face them eventually. My task completion rate of about 39% reflects these struggles.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have videos playing or materials open but my mind was elsewhere. High anxiety made it hard to concentrate even when I was technically 'present' in the LMS. I'd sometimes scroll through content without really absorbing it, just going through the motions. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they'd register as activity.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important—my 13% completion rate shows that clearly. Part of it was anxiety-driven avoidance; starting felt overwhelming. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better before attempting the assignment, which was sometimes true but sometimes just an excuse. My resilience eventually kicked in, but often later than it should have.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my motivator. They'd cause spikes in my anxiety but also force me into action when I might otherwise procrastinate. I'd often cram LMS activity into the days before something was due, which wasn't ideal but got things done. The structure of deadlines helped me prioritize among my 16 courses, even if it meant some things got last-minute attention.
Q17: Feedback had a significant impact on my engagement. Positive feedback on my assignments—I scored around 84% when I did submit—motivated me to keep trying despite my anxiety. Critical feedback sometimes triggered avoidance initially, but my resilience helped me eventually use it constructively. Knowing my work would be evaluated made me more careful about what I submitted, even if it meant submitting less overall.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation quite a bit. Having clear modules helped me plan my 30 study hours across topics. However, the volume of content across 16 courses sometimes made it hard to give each the attention it deserved. I appreciated when the structure allowed flexibility for my kinesthetic learning approach rather than forcing purely passive consumption.
Q19: The video content and peer interaction features made engagement easier—they matched my learning style and need for support. What made it harder was when too much was required in formats that didn't suit me, like extensive reading without application opportunities. The discussion forums were helpful but I felt anxious about posting publicly, so I engaged more through direct peer interactions instead.
Q20: I think that pattern could reflect someone like me who's exploring and trying to understand before committing to submissions. High anxiety might make someone engage extensively with materials but freeze when it comes to actually submitting work. They might also be perfectionists who keep reviewing but never feel ready to turn something in. Activity doesn't always equal output when psychological barriers are involved.
Q21: That definitely resonates with my experience. I performed reasonably well—Merit grade, decent exam scores—despite inconsistent LMS activity because so much of my real learning happened offline. Someone could download materials once and study them extensively without returning to the platform. Effective learning strategies don't always require constant online presence; sometimes less digital noise means more focus.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like login frequency, time spent on videos, and submission records—the quantitative side of engagement. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the anxiety someone's battling, or the peer support happening outside the platform. They can't capture whether someone's truly absorbing material or just going through motions. The numbers tell part of the story, not the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense and I can apply it. It's not just showing up or clicking through content—it's that feeling of connection when a concept clicks. For me, engagement involves hands-on practice, discussion with peers, and persistence through confusion. It's mental presence and emotional investment, not just physical or digital presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels and how hard I worked to manage them while studying. It wouldn't capture the peer support sessions that happened over coffee or through text messages. My offline practice sessions, the physical notes covering my desk, the real-world applications I tried—none of that appears in the data. Most importantly, it wouldn't show the resilience it took to keep going when everything felt overwhelming.
